summary: Stancil argues that the real danger of AI-powered surveillance isn't sophisticated spy technology but the automation of tedious data analysis that was previously too boring for anyone to bother doing. Drawing from his experience as a data analyst at an enterprise social network, he shows that our digital lives have always been thoroughly tracked—the only thing protecting our privacy was that analyzing the data required too much grunt work. AI removes that friction, making mass surveillance not a sci-fi scenario but a mundane inevitability.
key_points: All SaaS products already collect exhaustive behavioral data using standard tracking libraries—surveillance infrastructure has existed for years
Privacy has been protected not by encryption or security but by the tedium of analyzing messy, scattered data
AI's real threat to privacy isn't superintelligent pattern detection but automating the boring grunt work of data analysis at scale
Government agencies buy surveillance data on open markets that anyone else can also access
The assumption that nobody would bother watching us—the 'banality' defense—is collapsing as AI makes watching effortless
money_quotes: On an internet where everything is tracked—and man, everything is tracked—surveillance does not require a Ph.D., or even any particularly advanced math. It just requires a junior analyst with 24 hours of free time.
The real fences around the data all we leave behind—and the real protections of our privacy—are neither tall nor covered in barbed wire. They are simply fences that are annoying to climb.

Not of AI becoming a superintelligent Sherlock Holmes finding impossible patterns in its enormous mind palace, but of it being a million monkeys at a million typewriters, doing the grunt work no person wanted to do.
Because when prying questions are a prompt away—rather than 24 hours of work away—who wouldn't get tempted to pry?
Banality is a sturdy armor. Or was, anyway.
key_insight: AI doesn't need to be superintelligent to destroy privacy—it just needs to automate the tedious data work that was the only real barrier between our tracked lives and anyone curious enough to look.
